speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape 6 with Emil Jacoby-- also Jacobovitz. Today is December 18, 1995. OK, so you got to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I didn't get there. We just get off the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're heading toward the hospital on the street. And people go by, and they punch me. They saw a Jew. They come straight over, and they punch me up and curse me on the street. But the, the guard wouldn't go and protect me at that time already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, almost-- we are there at the hospital. All of a sudden, there was an air-raid siren. And people start running from the buses and from the-- from the cars, leaving everything on the street and running to a-- to a shelter underground, looking for shelters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my Ustase, my guard, also was running for it and says, come with me. But the crowd was so big that I got-- I got lost. And I looked around. I didn't see him already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go with the crowd. The crowd is running to-- they must go somewhere. So I was going with this crowd to a place. They went down into a shelter. I was going after them. And  I find myself in, in underground in a shelter in a dark place filled with people sitting next to me, sitting-- somebody with a gun in his hand. And I looked-- after I got used to the darkness already, I could-- I could make out that this is a German-- a German, with a uniform and a gun. And I took off, off slowly this yellow band from me. But he saw that I take off this yellow band. I didn't say nothing. He said to me-- he started cursing Hitler, the German, when, when, when this-- when it was-- we heard these detonations, these explosions in the city because the, the, the Americans obviously was bombing somewhere. And it was a big noise. And it was shaking. The whole building was shaking. He started cursing in German, God damn it, Hitler, [GERMAN]. He-- but he's causing my-- such a-- such a tragedy. My, my family-- I don't know for my family nothing. They're bombing for Germany, too, and this-- and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- I kept my mouth shut. Then he stopped cursing. And it was, was another siren calling off the, the, the alarm. And we got-- we stood up. And he wanted to fix his-- himself to go out like-- so he gave me this gun to hold his gun, you know? He didn't realize. But I didn't-- I was not even familiar how to pull, pull the trigger. I was just holding it like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he trusted me the gun-- a German, German soldier trusted to the Jew the gun. And I was, at the time, so emotionally-- so this is the end. This is the beginning of the end. If a German is cursing Hitler and he gives for the Jew a-- this is a signal that something is, is going on here. So I sneaked away from him without saying goodbye or something. I just started to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think he, he told me. I don't remember if, if, if I asked him. I think he said something like [? Fritz. ?] I was not sure-- maybe it's, it's, it's something-- I started to figure, maybe it's has something of meaning, the name. But no, it's not the same man which, which I used to-- which I met in Budapest, encountered there in the-- but so I was going and asking people how to get to the hospital. I came to the hospital. I found this Ustase there. And he got for me a, a belt for a-- a hernia belt. You know, this was pressing me in. Otherwise, my whole-- I've got here such a bump under my belly. I couldn't-- this, this belt was making me capable of working.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I worked. This was winter. I don't remember exactly when it was-- when it happened. But from then on, it was-- we reached, like, the end of March. We already heard from a distance at night-- we could hear the shell-- the shelling like, like, like thundering, you know? So we knew this must be the Russians. Because it's not raining. The weather is nice. And we hear this shelling-- these, these noises were like, like thundering-- boom. So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were waiting that something in that-- and a couple of days from then on, when we heard this noise, on the loudspeaker was orders giving everybody on the street to, to, to go in and, and, and, and, and line up-- each group separate-- each group by four or five people to line up. And we are moving. We have to move away, leave the whole camp. That was the repeating orders. So it was a big-- big turmoil was there. And we were happy that we were going. But we-- when we started to move, there was a lot of-- a lot of Gestapo on, on horses. There were-- there were thousands and thousands of, of people moving. There's a big camp. I didn't even realize how big this camp was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we moved. The one-- for a whole day  we were marching without stopping, without even making a break-- taking a break, all day. And we-- who couldn't make it-- who couldn't walk because he had to-- and he was stepped out, we heard shooting. We didn't-- I didn't see that he was-- he shoot this guy who moved, who, who left behind. But I imagined-- who else? They didn't shoot on rabbits. They shoot on people who couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were coming and yelling and saying to us, nobody steps out! Nobody falls back! We have to go moving in the same-- so we kept moving. And we helped each other. And we reached the city by the name of Fellbach on the main square. The whole camp was there-- was lining up, group by group by group. And there, I saw the whole people-- the Russians. I saw the French. And I saw all these were in this camp. Only we were Jewish-- Jewish about 200 people. We were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then came the other two Ustases. And he said to us, turn around. We marched again. It took us-- already it was dark. He took us in a direction. We saw on the sign, sign-- street sign that says direction of Linz, [POINTS] Mauthausen. [POINTS] These two names were on this-- on these signs in the direction of Mauthausen. So we walked for a while, for about half an hour. We left Fellbach, the city. We came to an open road. And both sides of the road, the woods were, were trees and hills, covered with, with, like, a wooden area on both sides. So they told us to take a break. Sit down, he says, take a break. We, too, want to go and-- inside the woods for a while. They wanted to relieve themselves or something. So we were watching them. It Was dark. All of a sudden, we didn't see them. We didn't hear them. We didn't see them. So our-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we started to ask, what is-- why is this happening? Where are they? What are we going to do? Doo, doo, doo-- we didn't know what to do. All of a sudden, somebody came up with an idea. Let's go and move up the hill in the-- among the trees and hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the Russians were supposed to be not far from here. These guards probably disappeared because they, they don't want to come with us. We're waiting half an hour. They didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to move up. Everybody was running and rushing in the dark. And sometimes, we didn't know where we were going. We were just going.

speaker: interviewee
text: I almost got lost because I, I didn't see nobody. I was going. And, and if this-- my friend wouldn't say to me, Emil, come, here, this way, this way, this way, I would lost-- got lost there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to a clearing of this hill. The trees are-- no trees. There was a clear area. And we saw that-- two-- there was-- I saw them-- a, a house and a barn-- a big barn. And we were saying, somebody should go inside and check it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went down, checked out the whole area. Nobody is inside. So we moved into this barn. It was half with, with straw and with, with hay. And he says, you cover yourself into this straw. If you hear something-- somebody coming, you cover up your head, too. We-- inside, only our head was sticking out from this-- from this straw that is inside. And we were there through the night-- the whole night. I was so tired that I fall-- sitting in this hay, it felt so good that I dozed off. I dozed off. All of a sudden, they wake me up-- shh!-- because one of the guys had to go out-- up the roof and watch. If somebody's coming, he have to tell us to cover ourselves. So he'd give us a, a sign. They are coming. Then we heard steps and, and, and, and, and noises and, and German voices speaking German. And the guy came down. And he went away. And he told us about nine-- a group of nine soldiers with anti-tank weapons were coming towards us. And they stopped. And they were talking. And then they moved further away. So I dozed off again. And around 2, 3 o'clock in the morning, I woke up to a terrible noise, like, you know, when it's lightning and, and thundering storm with an earthquake together. Both the earthquake-- the earth was shaking. Everything was shaking. Shells were exploding next to our-- next to our barn. We heard even the-- we heard the-- and, and, and, and bullets are fleeing. We heard the bullets flee through our ears. And we, we are hiding in this-- in the-- inside in the barn. And we were waiting any minute a shell should fall on our head and destroy-- and, and then kill us all. So this lasted for about till 6 o'clock in the morning-- three or four hours, this battle. We heard aircraft flying and, and shooting, and artillery shooting, and tanks, and everything. And then all of a sudden, it was-- quiet down. We had only-- we had only automatic fire. So we decided to send out somebody to look out and see what is going on. So from our-- from our roof, we could see down on the street, on the road between these hills-- tanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you couldn't know what kind of tanks these are. Two guys took a white-- a white-- each one took a white-- what is it-- a white shirt? Or a white-- one took a white towel to-- if he sees a German, he would shake this towel that he gives us up. And we cannot stay there long. So he goes. He goes. And we are waiting for almost an hour. Nobody comes back. All of a sudden, a guy says from upstairs-- from the roof-- he's coming. We are coming back. Who is coming back? We opened the door. They are knocking on the door. And who is there? These two guys with a Russian guy-- a Russian [LAUGHS] officer. And on that, we were so happy, we, we-- with that. And he says, you are free. And we were thanking him and kissing him and everything. And he says, where are you from? I says, we are from Czechoslovakia, from Hungary. He says, you see this city over there? You can go there and do whatever you want to do. Take everything you want to take. You are free. When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the 1st or the 2nd of April in 1945. So we went down the hill like, like animals, you see, ragged with this dress. And we saw almost every house-- the roof was either burning or the house was already burned down or, or, or, or destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were walking over debris of dead, dead soldiers-- Germans and Russians and all kind of dead people we were walking. We couldn't walk on the street inside the city, like, like-- it was so, so-- such a mess was there that we couldn't even walk there. And we were afraid  we'd step on something that could explode and all kind of things. So I went into a house. The, the roof was burning. I threw away my, my-- all my stuff, all my dresses. And I found in the-- living-- in the room-- I went to a-- to a-- and I found inside-- in the kitchen, I opened the refrigerator.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I ate something-- yogurt, bread. And then, I took-- I found-- I found a shirt, a clean shirt. And I took-- I think I took a shower, too, yes. And I  got dressed clean. And I came out. I had a bunch of-- I had-- I took a bag. And I stuffed there some, some, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some stuff like, like, yogurt, and milk, and canned food, and a piece of bread. And I went. And I went further. I went a little further. And I was stopped by a Russian. And they were looking-- do we have watches. They were looking for hand watches to take away. I didn't have. So I  showed them what I have. He says, OK, go. And that Russian who, who give us-- who came to, to, to liberate us, he had in his bag bread, his own bread. He took out his bread, and he gave everybody a piece of bread to eat-- his own bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were so hungry, we, we were-- we enjoyed this bread. And then in Fellbach-- in Fellbach, we, we-- I saw other guys running here, running there. They're looking for, for, for jewelry, for gold.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, they run in the house. And, and I saw my friend, [? Bela. ?] And he says, Emil, come, come, come, come. So I followed him into a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: We found there a little baby in the house. Nobody [LAUGHS] was in the house. All the Germans were escaped from the city-- or dead. And this little baby  was there. So they started to argue what to do-- to kill this baby or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw this argument. I left. I didn't want to even, even want to hear this argument because I will-- I could not bear to, to, to be there and see what they are going to do. And I don't know what they have done. Maybe they killed, maybe not. I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I had my-- I was thinking, why not kill it? Why not? Yes, kill, not kill. I was starting to argue with myself. Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, I started to go on the road. It was already dark. The road there was spanning in the city-- in this town. And then in the evening, I started to walk on the street. I didn't know where to go. Everybody goes towards Budapest because they're, they're looking for their homes. I have no home. I have nobody to go with-- to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you got to Budapest, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was going-- and then finally, I went to Budapest, yes. Because that was the closest-- the best way to do it. But I didn't, didn't know where to go. In Budapest, I came, and I found the city also very, very flattened with the fighting there for I don't know how many months. And it was hard to get by. I didn't recognize this area because everything was, was-- with the bombs, it was destroyed-- buildings and streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, I ended up in Bucharest. And Bucharest-- it was already a long time. The Russians were there already a whole, whole year. And Jewish organizations were there. So I joined a Jewish organization in Bucharest, a Zionist-- a Zionist-- What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-Halutz Hatzair. And being there in this group for  a while-- they came. And they, they announced that there's a-- the Jewish-- a Jewish international organization, like the [NON-ENGLISH] and the Jewish-- the United Jewish Appeals, and all of kinds of organization. They are organizing immigration-- illegal immigration to Palestine. There was still-- the British were there. We should sneak in, where they take us from a boat. Somewhere we had to-- we had small boats to, to sneak inside Palestine and to join a kibbutz or the army because we'll have to fight a war. This was 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- voluntarily, I said, I want to go. So I was in the first group of-- to take people who entirely went illegally to Palestine. I didn't know what else to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- I forgot to say. Into this-- in this kibbutz, where I was in Bucharest, I met my, my, my pal, [? Bela, ?] with whom is my school pal from, from Bustino. We went to school together. We were neighbors. And his father later I met in Israel. And he came back from Kamenetz-Podolskiy. And he told me the story of what happened in Bustino in 1941 when the deportation took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this Bela told me, Emil, why-- let's go back to Bustino. Why, why do we have to go to Palestine? You see? Then I find out that some guys came back to Bustino to see, for the last time, the hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the, the, the residents over there, the population, didn't welcome them. They just make up stories that the Jews came back to poison the wells, the water, because we had took over. We took away their property. And they wanted revenge. They wanted to  kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these-- the Russians arrested these Jewish guys who came back to see their homes. So we were afraid to go back home. And we-- rather-- I rather joined this illegal immigration group. They called it 'Aliyah Bet.' So they gave me a false passport with a-- with a phony-- a fake name. I don't remember the name. But the passport was from Switzerland. And they gave me some money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave us a list of protected houses where we should over-- sleep over when you get to a place. We cannot go to, to a hotel or to somebody. We have to go to a certain address who is from our people. And we show them this paper. They would let you sleep over. And they will give you further information where you had to go further.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- so we went like this till we reached Budapest-- by train, by trucks, by all kinds of-- all kinds of transportations. We slept over in many cities in Romania-- in [NON-ENGLISH], in Sighetu, and, and in, in all kinds of places we slept over in these houses-- until we reached again Budapest into this Jewish community building where I used to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, we were waiting to go from there to, to Vienna to pick up another people. In Budapest, we were supposed to pick up also some groups who are going illegally to Palestine. So I just snuck away from, from them for-- I said I have to visit somebody. And I went to see if my girlfriend is still there. And I came. And they said she didn't come home yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next day, I was-- we were there staying in Budapest for a couple of days. I went back again. And she was there. But I couldn't recognize it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was full of sores on her face. She was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] almost like a skinhead look, with no hair on her head, also with-- also with all kinds of sores, with all kind of-- she looked very skinny. And I couldn't recognize her. And she still had this uniform from, from the concentration camp where she was, with the-- and she was tattooed on her arm. She probably was in Auschwitz too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was-- she told me, listen, I-- where are you going? I told her, we're going to Palestine. We cannot stay here. There will come another war. And they will kill us, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said-- I cannot go like this, she says. If you wait here, when I will feel better-- and then I'll  be able to, to go and do something. But now I'm so very weak. I'm sick. I have to go to doctors, to hospitals. I cannot go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, you know what? I go now. You come and you join-- you come later. And we'll meet. And then we'll see what to do, OK? So I came back. And I-- we took a train-- the whole group-- a large group. From the three of us, we became group of a hundred-and-something people. So we took a train to Vienna. In  Vienna, we slept in-- also in a hotel. I don't remember the hotel we slept.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they told us in the morning-- early morning will come a truck. And they will take you over the, the zone between Vienna in the-- in the British zone, they say. No, we are in the Russian zone. Vienna was in the Russian zone. We take a-- we take a-- they're going to take us over to the British zone. We have to cross a river. And then we'll be in the British zone. In the British zone, they are going to take us in another trucks. They are going to take us to Italy. From Italy, they are going to take us to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Italy, you, you went to Italy. You told me  you were in a DP camp. What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The DP camp was Cinecitta. It was a-- before, it was a movie-- they'd make movies like in Hollywood. That was the Italian Hollywood, they called it. But it was big, large buildings. And there we will-- we slept. We took us there to, to, to-- no, no. They took us to a place in the south near, near, near a city called Bari. And the city-- the place was Maria-- Santa Maria di Bagni. And there I was-- I got-- I got-- I was given a job as an instructor in charge of a group of young boys and girls who came from the-- from the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when they decided that we have to move to Cyprus-- and I said, Cyprus? What? What's Cyprus? What am I going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cyprus is a British camp. We're going to be in that camp till the-- till the, the Jews will get independence. And then we'll go. We cannot go now there illegally further because the British are patrolling the, the ocean, the beaches. And they will catch us. And then we can get killed. We're better off waiting in this-- in this camp in Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, no way. I was in camps. I am not going to go now to another camp. I'm going to-- better go work and see in Italy what is going on and see the world. I'm free now. I'm going to go in a camp? Wait till, till Israel is going to be independent? I'm not going to do it. So I decided-- I took my, my bags. I boarded the train without any money. And I was going from, from wagon-- from car to car, so they wouldn't catch me for, for the-- for the-- for the ticket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes, I was caught without a ticket. And they took me off. And I went on another train. It took me a whole week to get from there to Rome. In Rome, I went to-- I went to Cinecitta. On the street, I found a-- an American jeep-- with a jeep. And I saw it's written there, UNRRA. And I came to him. And with  broken English, I told him the story that I am now a refugee from Germany. I don't have nobody. I want to go to America because I have an-- I have an aunt in America. I didn't know where she lives. But I know that she's an American.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he put me in this Cinecitta thing there. And there I was-- I was there sleeping, sleeping there and getting lunches and getting packages. And during the day, I was walking and seeing Rome-- Vatican I got to visit. All Rome-- all over Rome, I went to see all the places.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, I-- they asked me in the office about America-- to fill out forms. I couldn't remember the name of my aunt. So they said, if you don't know the name, you cannot find-- you have to know the name. Where is she located? What is her name? I don't remember. So I-- they asked me, do you want to go to-- you're from Czechoslovakia? Yes. There's a transport train leaving from here, from this camp, back to Czechoslovakia. I said, OK, I'll go. Why? Because I  want to see if somebody from my family survived. Maybe a brother survived, maybe my mother. Who knows? OK. So I joined this train. And it took me to Czechoslovakia. So you went back to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is it. That's It.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause again.
